[[ja:%E5%AE%87%E6%B2%BB%E6%A9%8B_(%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E5%B8%82)]] '''Uji Bridge (Ise)''' [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Uji Bridge (Ise City)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] <!--Uji Bridge (Ise City)-->[[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:goodbye $1]][[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:goodbye $1]] ]] {{Bridge | Name = Uji Bridge | Image = [[File:Uji-bashi 01.JPG|280px]] | Image_Caption = Uji Bridge | Country = {{JPN}} | City = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Crosses = {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} | Use = Road bridge | Maintained by = {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|31.5|N|136|43|23.5|E|region:JP-24|display=title}} | Length = 101.8m | Width = 8.42m | Architects = {{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}}, {{ill|Funadaiku|ja|船大工}}, {{ill|Hazama Corporation|ja|間組}} | Type = '''Japanese-style bridge (和橋)''' | Material = '''Wood (Hinoki cypress)''' | Built = '''November 3, 2009''' | Map = Japan Mie#Japan | Map_Size = 200px | Map_Caption = Location of Uji Bridge }}
'''Uji Bridge''' (宇治橋) is a '''wooden bridge''' in '''Ise City, Mie Prefecture, Japan''', located at the entrance of the '''approach (参道) to the Kōtai Jingū (皇大神宮, Inner Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine'''. It is also known as the '''"Mimosuso Bridge" (御裳濯橋)''' and is one of the '''Japan's 100 Famous Bridges (日本百名橋)'''<ref name="nikkei20200425">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO58358560S0A420C2W01000/ 日本経済新聞 (Nikkei), April 25, 2020] (Accessed: February 1, 2021).</ref>.
== Overview == [[File:Uji-bashi 06.jpg|thumb|240px|Photo taken in April 2010]]
Uji Bridge is a '''traditional wooden bridge (和橋, wakyō)''' with a length of '''101.8 meters''' and a width of '''8.42 meters''', spanning the '''Isuzu River''' at the entrance to the Inner Shrine of '''Ise Grand Shrine'''. A '''Shinmei-style torii (神明鳥居)''' is positioned at both ends of the bridge. The bridge serves as a '''symbol of the shrine''' and a '''popular spot for commemorative photos''' during visits<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20年ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge

After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''.

[[ja:%E5%AE%87%E6%B2%BB%E6%A9%8B_(%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E5%B8%82)]] '''Uji Bridge (Ise)''' [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Uji Bridge (Ise City)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] <!--Uji Bridge (Ise City)-->[[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]][[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]]]] {{Bridge | Name = Uji Bridge | Image = [[File:Uji-bashi 01.JPG|280px]] | Image_Caption = Uji Bridge | Country = {{JPN}} | City = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Crosses = {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} | Use = Road bridge | Maintained by = {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|31.5|N|136|43|23.5|E|region:JP-24|display=title}} | Length = 101.8m | Width = 8.42m | Architects = {{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}}, {{ill|Funadaiku|ja|船大工}}, {{ill|Hazama Corporation|ja|間組}} | Type = '''Japanese-style bridge (和橋)''' | Material = '''Wood (Hinoki cypress)''' | Built = '''November 3, 2009''' | Map = Japan Mie#Japan | Map_Size = 200px | Map_Caption = Location of Uji Bridge }}
'''Uji Bridge''' (宇治橋) is a '''wooden bridge''' in '''Ise City, Mie Prefecture, Japan''', located at the entrance of the '''approach (参道) to the Kōtai Jingū (皇大神宮, Inner Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine'''. It is also known as the '''"Mimosuso Bridge" (御裳濯橋)''' and is one of the '''Japan's 100 Famous Bridges (日本百名橋)'''<ref name="nikkei20200425">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO58358560S0A420C2W01000/ 日本経済新聞 (Nikkei), April 25, 2020] (Accessed: February 1, 2021).</ref>.
== Overview == [[File:Uji-bashi 06.jpg|thumb|240px|Photo taken in April 2010]]
Uji Bridge is a '''traditional wooden bridge (和橋, wakyō)''' with a length of '''101.8 meters''' and a width of '''8.42 meters''', spanning the '''Isuzu River''' at the entrance to the Inner Shrine of '''Ise Grand Shrine'''. A '''Shinmei-style torii (神明鳥居)''' is positioned at both ends of the bridge. The bridge serves as a '''symbol of the shrine''' and a '''popular spot for commemorative photos''' during visits<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20年ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge

According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20年ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]

[[ja:%E5%AE%87%E6%B2%BB%E6%A9%8B_(%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E5%B8%82)]] '''Uji Bridge (Ise)''' [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Uji Bridge (Ise City)}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] <!--Uji Bridge (Ise City)-->[[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]][[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]]]] {{Bridge | Name = Uji Bridge | image = [[File:Uji-bashi 01.JPG|280px]] | image_Caption = Uji Bridge | Country = {{JPN}} | City = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Crosses = {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} | Use = Road bridge | Maintained by = {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|31.5|N|136|43|23.5|E|region:JP-24|display=title}} | Length = 101.8m | Width = 8.42m | Architects = {{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}}, {{ill|Funadaiku|ja|船大工}}, {{ill|Hazama Corporation|ja|間組}} | Type = '''Japanese-style bridge (和橋)''' | Material = '''Wood (Hinoki cypress)''' | Built = '''November 3, 2009''' | Map = Japan Mie#Japan | Map_Size = 200px | Map_Caption = Location of Uji Bridge }}
'''Uji Bridge''' (宇治橋) is a '''wooden bridge''' in '''Ise City, Mie Prefecture, Japan''', located at the entrance of the '''approach (参道) to the Kōtai Jingū (皇大神宮, Inner Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine'''. It is also known as the '''"Mimosuso Bridge" (御裳濯橋)''' and is one of the '''Japan's 100 Famous Bridges (日本百名橋)'''<ref name="nikkei20200425">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO58358560S0A420C2W01000/ 日本経済新聞 (Nikkei), April 25, 2020] (Accessed: February 1, 2021).</ref>.
== Overview == [[File:Uji-bashi 06.jpg|thumb|240px|Photo taken in April 2010]]
Uji Bridge is a '''traditional wooden bridge (和橋, wakyō)''' with a length of '''101.8 meters''' and a width of '''8.42 meters''', spanning the '''Isuzu River''' at the entrance to the Inner Shrine of '''Ise Grand Shrine'''. A '''Shinmei-style torii (神明鳥居)''' is positioned at both ends of the bridge. The bridge serves as a '''symbol of the shrine''' and a '''popular spot for commemorative photos''' during visits<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20年ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge

revision.text:
[[ja:%E5%AE%87%E6%B2%BB%E6%A9%8B_(%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E5%B8%82)]] '''Uji Bridge (Ise)''' [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Uji Bridge (Ise City)}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] <!--Uji Bridge (Ise City)-->[[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]][[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]]]] {{Bridge | Name = Uji Bridge | image = [[File:Uji-bashi 01.JPG|280px]] | image_Caption = Uji Bridge | Country = {{JPN}} | City = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Crosses = {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} | Use = Road bridge | Maintained by = {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|31.5|N|136|43|23.5|E|region:JP-24|display=title}} | Length = 101.8m | Width = 8.42m | Architects = {{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}}, {{ill|Funadaiku|ja|船大工}}, {{ill|Hazama Corporation|ja|間組}} | Type = '''Japanese-style bridge (和橋)''' | Material = '''Wood (Hinoki cypress)''' | Built = '''November 3, 2009''' | Map = Japan Mie#Japan | Map_Size = 200px | Map_Caption = Location of Uji Bridge }}
'''Uji Bridge''' (宇治橋) is a '''wooden bridge''' in '''Ise City, Mie Prefecture, Japan''', located at the entrance of the '''approach (参道) to the Kōtai Jingū (皇大神宮, Inner Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine'''. It is also known as the '''"Mimosuso Bridge" (御裳濯橋)''' and is one of the '''Japan's 100 Famous Bridges (日本百名橋)'''<ref name="nikkei20200425">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO58358560S0A420C2W01000/ 日本経済新聞 (Nikkei), April 25, 2020] (Accessed: February 1, 2021).</ref>.
== Overview == [[File:Uji-bashi 06.jpg|thumb|240px|Photo taken in April 2010]]
Uji Bridge is a '''traditional wooden bridge (和橋, wakyō)''' with a length of '''101.8 meters''' and a width of '''8.42 meters''', spanning the '''Isuzu River''' at the entrance to the Inner Shrine of '''Ise Grand Shrine'''. A '''Shinmei-style torii (神明鳥居)''' is positioned at both ends of the bridge. The bridge serves as a '''symbol of the shrine''' and a '''popular spot for commemorative photos''' during visits<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20年ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''.
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]

== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]

revision.text:
[[ja:%E5%AE%87%E6%B2%BB%E6%A9%8B_(%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E5%B8%82)]] '''Uji Bridge (Ise)''' [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Uji Bridge (Ise City)}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] <!--Uji Bridge (Ise City)-->[[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]][[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]]]] {{Bridge | Name = Uji Bridge | image = [[File:Uji-bashi 01.JPG|280px]] | image_Caption = Uji Bridge | Country = {{JPN}} | City = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Crosses = {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} | Use = Road bridge | Maintained by = {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|31.5|N|136|43|23.5|E|region:JP-24|display=title}} | Length = 101.8m | Width = 8.42m | Architects = {{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}}, {{ill|Funadaiku|ja|船大工}}, {{ill|Hazama Corporation|ja|間組}} | Type = '''Japanese-style bridge (和橋)''' | Material = '''Wood (Hinoki cypress)''' | Built = '''November 3, 2009''' | Map = Japan Mie#Japan | Map_Size = 200px | Map_Caption = Location of Uji Bridge }}
'''Uji Bridge''' (宇治橋) is a '''wooden bridge''' in '''Ise City, Mie Prefecture, Japan''', located at the entrance of the '''approach (参道) to the Kōtai Jingū (皇大神宮, Inner Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine'''. It is also known as the '''"Mimosuso Bridge" (御裳濯橋)''' and is one of the '''Japan's 100 Famous Bridges (日本百名橋)'''<ref name="nikkei20200425">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO58358560S0A420C2W01000/ 日本経済新聞 (Nikkei), April 25, 2020] (Accessed: February 1, 2021).</ref>.
== Overview == [[File:Uji-bashi 06.jpg|thumb|240px|Photo taken in April 2010]]
Uji Bridge is a '''traditional wooden bridge (和橋, wakyō)''' with a length of '''101.8 meters''' and a width of '''8.42 meters''', spanning the '''Isuzu River''' at the entrance to the Inner Shrine of '''Ise Grand Shrine'''. A '''Shinmei-style torii (神明鳥居)''' is positioned at both ends of the bridge. The bridge serves as a '''symbol of the shrine''' and a '''popular spot for commemorative photos''' during visits<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20年ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''.== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]

[[ja:%E5%AE%87%E6%B2%BB%E6%A9%8B_(%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E5%B8%82)]] '''Uji Bridge (Ise)''' [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Uji Bridge (Ise City)}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] <!--Uji Bridge (Ise City)-->[[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]][[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]]]] {{Bridge | Name = Uji Bridge | image = [[File:Uji-bashi 01.JPG|280px]] | image_Caption = Uji Bridge | Country = {{JPN}} | City = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Crosses = {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} | Use = Road bridge | Maintained by = {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|31.5|N|136|43|23.5|E|region:JP-24|display=title}} | Length = 101.8m | Width = 8.42m | Architects = {{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}}, {{ill|Funadaiku|ja|船大工}}, {{ill|Hazama Corporation|ja|間組}} | Type = '''Japanese-style bridge (和橋)''' | Material = '''Wood (Hinoki cypress)''' | Built = '''November 3, 2009''' | Map = Japan Mie#Japan | Map_Size = 200px | Map_Caption = Location of Uji Bridge }}

This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20年ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''. == See Also! == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]

[[ja:%E5%AE%87%E6%B2%BB%E6%A9%8B_(%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E5%B8%82)]] '''Uji Bridge (Ise)''' [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Uji Bridge (Ise City)}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] <!--Uji Bridge (Ise City)-->[[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]][[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]]]] {{Bridge | Name = Uji Bridge | image = [[File:Uji-bashi 01.JPG|280px]] | image_Caption = Uji Bridge | Country = {{JPN}} | City = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Crosses = {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} | Use = Road bridge | Maintained by = {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|31.5|N|136|43|23.5|E|region:JP-24|display=title}} | Length = 101.8m | Width = 8.42m | Architects = {{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}}, {{ill|Funadaiku|ja|船大工}}, {{ill|Hazama Corporation|ja|間組}} | Type = '''Japanese-style bridge (和橋)''' | Material = '''Wood (Hinoki cypress)''' | Built = '''November 3, 2009''' | Map = Japan Mie#Japan | Map_Size = 200px | Map_Caption = Location of Uji Bridge }}
'''Uji Bridge''' (宇治橋) is a '''wooden bridge''' in '''Ise City, Mie Prefecture, Japan''', located at the entrance of the '''approach (参道) to the Kōtai Jingū (皇大神宮, Inner Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine'''. It is also known as the '''"Mimosuso Bridge" (御裳濯橋)''' and is one of the '''Japan's 100 Famous Bridges (日本百名橋)'''<ref name="nikkei20200425">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO58358560S0A420C2W01000/ 日本経済新聞 (Nikkei), April 25, 2020] (Accessed: February 1, 2021).</ref>.
== Overview == [[File:Uji-bashi 06.jpg|thumb|240px|Photo taken in April 2010]]
Uji Bridge is a '''traditional wooden bridge (和橋, wakyō)''' with a length of '''101.8 meters''' and a width of '''8.42 meters''', spanning the '''Isuzu River''' at the entrance to the Inner Shrine of '''Ise Grand Shrine'''. A '''Shinmei-style torii (神明鳥居)''' is positioned at both ends of the bridge. The bridge serves as a '''symbol of the shrine''' and a '''popular spot for commemorative photos''' during visits<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20年ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''. == See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]

[[ja:%E5%AE%87%E6%B2%BB%E6%A9%8B_(%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E5%B8%82)]] '''Uji Bridge (Ise)''' [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Uji Bridge (Ise City)}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Uji Bridge (Ise City)]][[Category:11]] <!--Uji Bridge (Ise City)-->[[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]][[Category:Uji Bridge (Ise)]]]] {{Bridge | Name = Uji Bridge | image = [[File:Uji-bashi 01.JPG|280px]] | image_Caption = Uji Bridge | Country = {{JPN}} | City = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Crosses = {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} | Use = Road bridge | Maintained by = {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|31.5|N|136|43|23.5|E|region:JP-24|display=title}} | Length = 101.8m | Width = 8.42m | Architects = {{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工}}, {{ill|Funadaiku|ja|船大工}}, {{ill|Hazama Corporation|ja|間組}} | Type = '''Japanese-style bridge (和橋)''' | Material = '''Wood (Hinoki cypress)''' | Built = '''November 3, 2009''' | Map = Japan Mie#Japan | Map_Size = 200px | Map_Caption = Location of Uji Bridge }}
'''Uji Bridge''' (宇治橋) is a '''wooden bridge''' in '''Ise City, Mie Prefecture, Japan''', located at the entrance of the '''approach (参道) to the Kōtai Jingū (皇大神宮, Inner Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine'''. It is also known as the '''"Mimosuso Bridge" (御裳濯橋)''' and is one of the '''Japan's 100 Famous Bridges (日本百名橋)'''<ref name="nikkei20200425">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO58358560S0A420C2W01000/ 日本経済新聞 (Nikkei), April 25, 2020] (Accessed: February 1, 2021).</ref>.
== Overview == [[File:Uji-bashi 06.jpg|thumb|240px|Photo taken in April 2010]]
Uji Bridge is a '''traditional wooden bridge (和橋, wakyō)''' with a length of '''101.8 meters''' and a width of '''8.42 meters''', spanning the '''Isuzu River''' at the entrance to the Inner Shrine of '''Ise Grand Shrine'''. A '''Shinmei-style torii (神明鳥居)''' is positioned at both ends of the bridge. The bridge serves as a '''symbol of the shrine''' and a '''popular spot for commemorative photos''' during visits<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20th year ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''. == See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]

== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''. == See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]

revision.text:
'''Uji Bridge (Ise)'''
<!--Uji Bridge (Ise City)-->]] {{Bridge | Name = Uji Bridge | image = [[File:Uji-bashi 01.JPG|280px]] | image_Caption = Uji Bridge | Country = {{JPN}} | City = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} | Crosses = {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft}} | Use = Road bridge | Maintained by = {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|31.5|N|136|43|23.5|E|region:JP-24|display=title}} | Length = 101.8m | Width = 8.42m | Architects = {{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工|12=draft}}, {{ill|Funadaiku|ja|船大工|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hazama Corporation|ja|間組|12=draft}} | Type = '''Japanese-style bridge (和橋)''' | Material = '''Wood (Hinoki cypress)''' | Built = '''November 3, 2009''' | Map = Japan Mie#Japan | Map_Size = 200px | Map_Caption = Location of Uji Bridge }}
'''Uji Bridge''' (宇治橋) is a '''wooden bridge''' in '''Ise City, Mie Prefecture, Japan''', located at the entrance of the '''approach (参道) to the Kōtai Jingū (皇大神宮, Inner Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine'''. It is also known as the '''"Mimosuso Bridge" (御裳濯橋)''' and is one of the '''Japan's 100 Famous Bridges (日本百名橋)'''<ref name="nikkei20200425">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO58358560S0A420C2W01000/ 日本経済新聞 (Nikkei), April 25, 2020] (Accessed: February 1, 2021).</ref>.
== Overview == [[File:Uji-bashi 06.jpg|thumb|240px|Photo taken in April 2010]]
Uji Bridge is a '''traditional wooden bridge (和橋, wakyō)''' with a length of '''101.8 meters''' and a width of '''8.42 meters''', spanning the '''Isuzu River''' at the entrance to the Inner Shrine of '''Ise Grand Shrine'''. A '''Shinmei-style torii (神明鳥居)''' is positioned at both ends of the bridge. The bridge serves as a '''symbol of the shrine''' and a '''popular spot for commemorative photos''' during visits<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20th year ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''. == See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅|12=draft}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町|12=draft}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり|12=draft}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]

revision.text:
'''Uji Bridge (Ise)''' <!--Uji Bridge (Ise City)-->]] {{Bridge | Name = Uji Bridge | image = [[File:Uji-bashi 01.JPG|280px]] | image_Caption = Uji Bridge | Country = {{JPN}} | City = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} | Crosses = {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft}} | Use = Road bridge | Maintained by = {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|31.5|N|136|43|23.5|E|region:JP-24|display=title}} | Length = 101.8m | Width = 8.42m | Architects = {{ill|Miyadaiku|ja|宮大工|12=draft}}, {{ill|Funadaiku|ja|船大工|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hazama Corporation|ja|間組|12=draft}} | Type = '''Japanese-style bridge (和橋)''' | Material = '''Wood (Hinoki cypress)''' | Built = '''November 3, 2009''' | Map = Japan Mie#Japan | Map_Size = 200px | Map_Caption = Location of Uji Bridge }}
'''Uji Bridge''' (宇治橋) is a '''wooden bridge''' in '''Ise City, Mie Prefecture, Japan''', located at the entrance of the '''approach (参道) to the Kōtai Jingū (皇大神宮, Inner Shrine) of Ise Grand Shrine'''. It is also known as the '''"Mimosuso Bridge" (御裳濯橋)''' and is one of the '''Japan's 100 Famous Bridges (日本百名橋)'''<ref name="nikkei20200425">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO58358560S0A420C2W01000/ 日本経済新聞 (Nikkei), April 25, 2020] (Accessed: February 1, 2021).</ref>.
== Overview == [[File:Uji-bashi 06.jpg|thumb|240px|Photo taken in April 2010]]
Uji Bridge is a '''traditional wooden bridge (和橋, wakyō)''' with a length of '''101.8 meters''' and a width of '''8.42 meters''', spanning the '''Isuzu River''' at the entrance to the Inner Shrine of '''Ise Grand Shrine'''. A '''Shinmei-style torii (神明鳥居)''' is positioned at both ends of the bridge. The bridge serves as a '''symbol of the shrine''' and a '''popular spot for commemorative photos''' during visits<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20th year ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''. == See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川|12=draft}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅|12=draft}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町|12=draft}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり|12=draft}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]

It is also known as the '''"Mimosuso Bridge" (御裳濯橋)''' and is one of the '''Japan's 100 Famous Bridges (日本百名橋)'''<ref name="nikkei20200425">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO58358560S0A420C2W01000/ 日本経済新聞 (Nikkei), April 25, 2020] (Accessed: February 1, 2021).</ref>.
== Overview == [[File:Uji-bashi 06.jpg|thumb|240px|Photo taken in April 2010]]
Uji Bridge is a '''traditional wooden bridge (和橋, wakyō)''' with a length of '''101.8 meters''' and a width of '''8.42 meters''', spanning the '''Isuzu River''' at the entrance to the Inner Shrine of '''Ise Grand Shrine'''. A '''Shinmei-style torii (神明鳥居)''' is positioned at both ends of the bridge. The bridge serves as a '''symbol of the shrine''' and a '''popular spot for commemorative photos''' during visits<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
According to the shrine’s definition, '''the area beyond Uji Bridge is not yet the sacred precinct (神域), but rather the outer garden (神苑)'''. However, visitors and worshippers generally '''regard the bridge as marking the boundary between the mundane and the sacred'''<ref name="nikkei20200425"/>.
Since the '''Meiji period''', Uji Bridge has been '''rebuilt in conjunction with the Shikinen Sengū (式年遷宮) of Ise Shrine''', which occurs every '''20 years'''. Following '''Japan’s defeat in World War II''', '''Emperor Shōwa (昭和天皇)''' ordered an indefinite postponement of the '''59th Shikinen Sengū''', which was later held in '''1953 (Shōwa 28)''', four years behind schedule. However, due to the strong demand for rebuilding the bridge, '''Uji Bridge alone was reconstructed in 1949 (Shōwa 24)''', establishing the practice of '''rebuilding the bridge four years before the main shrine structures'''. This schedule '''reduces the number of required carpenters''', '''prolongs public interest in the Sengū''', and '''helps secure funding for the shrine’s reconstruction'''.
== The Torii of Uji Bridge == The '''torii''' at both ends of Uji Bridge measure '''7.44 meters''' in height and '''70 cm''' in diameter at their thickest point, with a total weight of approximately '''5 tons'''. The '''west torii''' is crafted from '''old cypress pillars''' of the '''Outer Shrine (Gekū) Main Hall''', while the '''east torii''' is made from '''pillars of the Inner Shrine (Naikū) Main Hall'''<ref name="ik1410">{{Cite web|url=http://iseshima.keizai.biz/headline/2142/|publisher=伊勢志摩経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮宇治橋前の大鳥居完成|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=December 29, 2014}}</ref>.
As part of the '''Shikinen Sengū''', the torii are replaced every '''20 years'''. During the '''62nd Shikinen Sengū''', the new torii was completed on '''October 3, 2014'''<ref name="nk1410">{{Cite web|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASDG0300R_T01C14A0CC0000/|publisher=日本経済新聞|title=伊勢神宮の宇治橋鳥居、20th year ぶり新調|date=October 3, 2014|accessdate=February 1, 2021}}</ref>.
The '''exact date when the first torii was installed is unknown''', but historical paintings from the '''late Muromachi period''' already depict the bridge with torii<ref name="nk1410"/>.
After being replaced, the '''old torii are repurposed''': the '''west torii is sent to Kuwana’s "Shichiri-no-Watashi" ferry site''', while the '''east torii is relocated to the "Seki no Higashi Oiwake" site in Kameyama City'''. After 20 years, the wood is '''recycled for use in shrines across Japan'''<ref name="ik1410"/>.
== Sunrise Phenomenon == [[File:Uji-bashi 03.JPG|thumb|180px|Sunrise in mid-December]]
Uji Bridge is aligned '''almost perpendicular to the Isuzu River''', with the '''east side slightly tilted southward by about 30 degrees'''. Due to the presence of '''Mt. Shimanji (島路山) on the eastern horizon''', the '''sun rises from behind the mountain'''.
During the '''winter solstice (December 22) and the surrounding months''', the '''sun appears directly between the torii''', creating a '''famous sunrise viewing phenomenon'''. This event '''was only noticed in the 1980s''' and gained popularity after being featured in '''Ise Shrine’s official publication, "Mizugaki" (瑞垣)'''.
On '''December 22, 2014''', approximately '''600 people''' gathered at the shrine to witness the '''solstice sunrise'''<ref name="cn1412">中日新聞, December 23, 2014 (伊勢志摩版)</ref>.
== Uji Bridge Watarizome-shiki Ceremony == When a '''new Uji Bridge is completed''', a special '''"First Crossing Ceremony" (宇治橋渡始式, Uji-bashi Watarizome-shiki)''' is held to '''bless the bridge and perform the inaugural crossing'''.
Traditionally, '''long-lived elderly individuals''' were chosen for the '''first crossing''', but since '''1823 (Bunsei 6)''', inspired by a custom at the '''Ryōgoku Bridge (両国橋)''' in Edo, the honor has been given to '''a three-generation family couple'''. == See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Akafuku|ja|赤福餅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – a famous Ise sweet * '''Takeshi Tsuji''' (竹内浩三) – wrote the poem *"Uji Bridge"* * {{ill|Oharaimachi|ja|おはらい町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – a traditional street extending north from Uji Bridge * {{ill|Ise Meguri|ja|伊勢めぐり|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – a song by '''Kaori Mizumori (水森かおり)''' that mentions Uji Bridge
== External Links == {{commonscat|Uji-bashi (Naiku)}} * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/naiku/ujibashi.html Ise Grand Shrine Official Site – Uji Bridge]
